data.title: Marfrig Global Foods Will Need Packaged Food Growth To Offset Commodity Pressures
data.description:
Cyclicality is a fact of life in commodity businesses, and Marfrig Global Foods (OTCPK:MBRFY) has ample experience with the ups and downs of the global protein cycles. With the U.S. beef business likely to be in a tough spot for a few years due to high costs and a slow rebuild of herds and the acquired BRF poultry business likely facing a cyclical peak, Marfrig will need to lean heavily on the growing packaged foods business that BRF brought with it in this year’s merger.

Valuation is a bit complicated at this point, as below-trend margins in the near term complicate multiples-based approaches, and long-term free cash flow modeling is challenging at best given the cyclicality of the global protein markets. I do think that Marfrig is undervalued, and potentially meaningfully so if the company’s cost synergy and packaged food efforts pan out, but the next 12-24 months are likely to see meaningful headwinds for the business.

Q3 Results Were Decent, Given The Circumstances

The fiscal third quarter marked the first for the new Marfrig, and all things considered, it was a respectable performance in a tough environment.

Revenue rose 9% year over year and 7% quarter over quarter, with double-digit growth in the North American (up 12% yoy) and South American (up 18% yoy) beef businesses and 5% growth in the BRF operations. Volume was down about 6% in North American beef, but offset by strong realized pricing (up 20%) as beef prices have shot up in response to supply challenges.

While the BRF business was hampered by restrictions on poultry imports from Brazil in China and the EU (due to bird flu), domestic demand was healthy on an improving economy, demand from markets like Saudi Arabia and Korea helped offset some of the import restrictions, and packaged food sales grew about 7% as the company continued to gain share.

Margins were less positive. Beef packing gross profits are well below their long-term average across the sector on higher cattle prices, and Marfrig saw gross margin in the North American business fall 90 bps to 3.6%. Overall gross margin fell 160 bps to 12.3%, with South American gross margin down 70 bps to 16.4% and BRF down 260 bps to 24.7%.

This fed through to the EBITDA line, with adjusted EBITDA down almost 9% yoy (margin down 160 bps to 8.4%). North American EBITDA was down 6% yoy (margin down 40 bps to 2%), and BRF EBITDA was down 15% (margin down 370 bps to 15.5%), while South American EBITDA was up almost 32% (with margin up 120 bps to 11.1%).

Debt is a meaningful consideration here, with the company ending the quarter with a 3.1x net debt-to-EBITDA ratio.

Beef Likely To Be Pressured For Some Time

The U.S. beef market is not in a great place right now, with volumes down on shrinking herds (the July U.S. cattle herd was the smallest in more than 50 years) and correspondingly lower slaughter levels. That has sent cattle prices much higher (up almost 27% recently), and put pressure on packers like Marfrig, JBS (JBS), and Tyson (TSN) as realized prices aren’t keeping pace.

To that end, the recent U.S. decision to roll back tariffs on Brazilian beef won’t be any real help to Marfrig or JBS.

Likewise, biology limits how quickly the herd can be rebuilt, and it’s likely going to take around two or three years for Marfrig’s business to get back to more normal EBITDA margins (around 6%).

The Poultry Cycle Could Be More Supportive Than Previously Thought

As I’ve written in the past in reference to BRF, the global chicken market is likely peaking, and that could pressure results in 2026 and beyond. Lower feed prices have been supporting producer margins, and poultry’s relative affordability has been helping demand (particularly in light of higher prices for other proteins), but countries like the Philippines, Thailand, Ukraine, and Vietnam have been increasing their export volumes at a pace that threatens prices and margins next year (not to mention growth in volumes from the U.S. and Brazil).

While the global poultry market may not be so strong next year, Marfrig’s particular experience could be a little better. For starters, the company enjoys a good share in Turkey and the Middle East, and further growth here (including a recent joint venture with the Saudi Arabian sovereign wealth fund called Sadia Halal) should be a positive for the business.

I also believe the Brazilian poultry market could be in better shape next year. A healthy economy (including recent record-low unemployment) has made poultry more affordable for more people, and capacity constraints for slaughtering and cold storage in Brazil are likely to limit slaughter volume growth to only 1% or 2% next year. While companies are investing in capacity, it takes time to build and come on line, so the next 12-18 months are looking pretty good for Marfrig.

Packaged/Processed Food Remains The Key Longer-Term Driver

In the long years that I followed BRF (and before that, Sadia), the company’s packaged/processed food business was always an important part of the story, and that is no less true now post the merger with Marfrig.

Processed foods are about 30% of the revenue base, and Marfrig enjoys a strong share in Brazil (over 41%), as well as markets like Turkey and the Middle East. Processed food margins can be double those of unprocessed proteins (or more), and growth here will help offset some of the near-term pressures on the beef business and drive higher overall margins over the long term. With the company investing meaningfully to further expand its processed food capacity, I do expect this to be a significant growth driver, and Marfrig may be able to leverage BRF’s brands into new markets over time.

The Outlook

Given the pressures on the beef and poultry businesses, I’m not expecting a lot of revenue growth over the next few years, but I do think global protein demand growth, expanded processed food volumes, and market share gains can drive respectable long-term revenue growth in the 4% to 5% range.

On the margin side, I do expect adverse beef and poultry cycles to create great margin pressures in 2026-27. Some of that can be offset by further growth in the processed food business, but it also puts more pressure on management to hit their R$1B cost synergy target for the Marfrig-BRF deal. While I do think double-digit EBITDA margins could be possible over the long term, I’m not modeling that at this point, and I’m expecting margins in the 7%-8% range for a couple of years.

Looking at free cash flow, I don’t expect the company to achieve 4%+ FCF margins on a sustained basis given the pressures from commodity cycles, but there will likely be a few years comfortably above (and below) that level. Long term, I’m expecting FCF margins to average out in the 3%’s, driving mid-single-digit FCF growth (around 6% or so).

Discounted cash flows suggest that Marfrig could be meaningfully undervalued and priced for over 10% long-term annualized total returns, but there’s significant uncertainty when modeling out a commodity business beyond a few years. On the flip side, the weaker near-term outlook for margins makes multiple-based approaches (EV/EBITDA, P/E, et al) challenging as well, since the near-term earnings aren’t really representative of the company’s earning power. In any case, a blended forward multiple of 5.75x (weighing in both the higher-multiple packaged food business and lower-multiple unprocessed protein businesses) on my 2028 EBITDA discounted back two years gets me a fair value of around $4/share.

The Bottom Line

With the shares up about a third from their mid-October lows, Marfrig shares are in a weird place. I think the outlook for U.S. beef and global poultry over the next 12 months is challenging, and odds are that this stock will have above-average volatility. I think the business mix is solid, though, and I can see a case for a higher valuation over time. Given a fair bit of negativity in sentiment already, I think this is a name that patient, value-oriented investors could consider here.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Cellebrite DI: Strong Q3, But Valuation And Technical Issues Could Weigh On The Stock (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
This article is ongoing coverage of Cellebrite DI (CLBT), which I last wrote about in November 2024. In my last article, I discussed how Cellebrite had strong long-term growth potential. I also explained that the valuation increase and the technical set-up could lead to a profit-taking pullback. It looks like the higher valuation and the technical level led to a sell-off from February to July 2025. The stock has dropped 9% since my last article.

Cellebrite's Q3 2025 earnings results provide insights into the company's potential performance. I currently see a potential issue again with the valuation. The technical level also looks bearish, which could lead to continued selling in the stock.

Cellebrite's Q3 2025 Results

Cellebrite did beat analysts' estimates for the top and bottom lines in Q3 2025. Revenue increased 18% yoy to $126 million, beating estimates by $3 million. Earnings per share remained flat yoy at $0.14, but beat analysts' estimates by $0.01.

Cellebrite's total revenue increased 18% yoy to $126 million in Q3 2025. This was a result of a 21% gain in subscription revenue. Subscription-based software solutions comprised about 89% of total revenue.

CLBT's annual recurring revenue [ARR] increased 19% yoy to $440 million in Q3 2025. This gain was attributed to increased spending by Cellebrite's existing customers. Cellebrite also achieved a 5% sequential gain over Q2 2025 for ARR. Geographically, the Americas comprised 55% of ARR, the EMEA comprised 33%, and Asia Pacific comprised 12%.

The gross profit increased 15.6% to $106.5 million. However, the gross margin declined from 85.6% in Q3 2024 down to 84.9% in Q3 2025 due to a 31.5% YoY increase in the cost of revenue. CLBT pointed out in the Q3 conference call that these results reflected the ongoing investment in cloud infrastructure as they rolled out Guardian and invested in the federal ATO process.

The operating margin declined from 18.2% in Q3 2024 to 15% in Q3 2025. However, the net income margin increased from a negative (194%) up to a positive 16% over the same time. The difference in the net income margin was a result of a large one-time interest expense of $224 million in Q3 2024.

Adjusted EBITDA increased 20% yoy to $37.7 million, while the adjusted EBITDA margin increased to 29.9% over the 29% from Q3 2024. This growth was achieved through disciplined hiring activity and strategic capital allocation across the organization.

Cellebrite experienced more customers turning to its cloud and SaaS offerings to manage their digital forensics and investigative workflows. CLBT pointed out in the Q3 conference call that its Guardian solution is emerging as the industry standard for the storage and collaboration of sensitive evidential artifacts.

The number of Guardian customers more than doubled YoY as ARR increased by at least 100% for the 5th consecutive quarter. The company can add to this growth as it will launch Guardian Investigate (an AI-powered investigative software designed for investigative teams to resolve cases faster) in Q1 2026.

Cellebrite narrowed its guidance for full year revenue. The company expects revenue to be in the range of $470 million to $475 million for 2025. This is a narrower range than CLBT's previous range of $465 million to $475 million at the end of Q2 2025. The analysts' consensus estimate is $473 million. CLBT also increased its guidance for adjusted EBITDA to be $124 million to $127 million. This was higher than the range given at the end of Q2 which was $118 million to $123 million.

Cellebrite's Growth Drivers

Cellebrite has multiple factors that can drive long-term growth. The launch of Guardian Investigate that I previously mentioned is one of these drivers. The company is targeting a launch in Q1 2026. By speeding up the process to resolve cases, CLBT can help investigative teams to save time and money.

CLBT sees expansion opportunities ahead for Inseyets, the company's digital forensics software suite. Cellebrite expects to drive broader adoption for Inseyets by extending their reach to new user groups and by upselling advanced unlock solutions and automation offerings. The company also expects to win new logos and to increase price or mix on existing operators. CLBT expects these growth drivers to result in high single-digit percentage growth in 2026.

Cellebrite also expects its Guardian and Pathfinder solutions to act as growth drivers. The company is anticipating healthy demand for Guardian from its core markets. CLBT also expects to expand Guardian in the U.S. Federal sector, in Australia and other international markets, and from sales to enterprises. The company also expects to achieve new business expansion and a stronger renewal rate for Pathfinder. These two solutions are expected to contribute mid single-digit ARR percentage growth.

The Corellium acquisition is another future growth driver. Cellebrite announced its intention to acquire Corellium back in June 2025. Corellium is a leader in Arm-based virtualization software. This will add to Cellebrite's current offerings. CLBT plans to sell Corellium's solutions to its existing customer base in the defense, enterprise, and intelligence categories. The acquisition is expected to close during Q4 2025 according to Cellebrite. CLBT expects Corellium to increase the company's total addressable market [TAM] and total value proposition. Corellium is expected to add a couple of percentage points to CLBT's AAR growth rate, while costing the company $150 million to $160 million for the purchase.

Valuation

I have concerns since Cellebrite's valuation level increased over the past year. I used the PEG ratio since that takes multiple years of earnings growth into the equation. Plus, Cellebrite has been a strong growth company with double-digit revenue and earnings growth. CLBT's PEG ratio increased from 1.72 in November 2024 when I last wrote about the company, up to the current level of 2.08. This is higher than the sector median PEG of 1.57. I typically like to see the PEG ratio below two.

Adding to the overvaluation is the forward PE ratio of 34x, which is much higher than the sector median of 22x. The forward price/sales of 9x is also much higher than the sector median of 3. The stock may lag the broader market with its valuation at these high levels.

CLBT is expected to grow earnings at 21% in 2025, but follow that up with a lower growth rate of 7.4% in 2026 (consensus). The lower growth rate for 2026 is likely the result of paying for the Corellium acquisition. These earnings growth rates can be achieved with the expected revenue growth of about 18% for 2025 and about 17% for 2026. The company's net income margin expansion that I mentioned earlier can drive earnings to hit the expected target for 2025.

Technical Perspective

We can see that CLBT's stock gapped up after the Q3 earnings report. However, the stock has dropped from about $20 down to the current price of $16.81 since then. The RSI indicator turned bearish as it moved below the 50 level. The MACD is not yet bearish since the blue MACD line remains above the red signal line. The MACD histogram bars are declining in length, suggesting waning momentum. The MACD would officially turn bearish when the blue line crosses below the red signal line while the histogram bars turn back to red.

I zoomed out to Cellebrite's weekly chart above. This shows the stock failing to rise above $21 back in March and failing to rise above $20 earlier this November. We now have two lower highs in the stock price, which looks bearish. The RSI is also bearish on the weekly chart as it fell back below the 50 level. The MACD looks like it is about to turn bearish as the blue line appears close to crossing below the red signal line. The histogram bars have been declining in length, suggesting the waning momentum could cause the bars to turn red below the zero line if the bearish crossover occurs.

Given the high valuation and this technical weakness, I think the most likely scenario for the stock is continued selling over the next few weeks or months. At the very least, I expect the stock to lag the broader market in the near term due to the stock's overvaluation.

Additional Risks for the Stock

Competition remains an ongoing threat to Cellebrite. Competition is intense for these types of software solutions. Many competitors are larger than Cellebrite, which means they have more financial and other resources to expand, develop products, and make acquisitions. Consolidation in the industry could result in pricing pressures. Competition could make it difficult for Cellebrite to grow at a strong pace in the future.

Cellebrite depends on obtaining business from law enforcement and government agencies. CLBT derived 90% of revenue from law enforcement and government agencies in 2022, 2023, and 2024. Less spending from these customers could significantly harm Cellebrite revenue.

The company depends on the renewal of subscriptions of its offerings. Reductions in subscriptions could have a negative impact on revenue and earnings. The company may also struggle to add new subscribers to its client list.

The Outlook For Cellebrite

I do think that Cellebrite had a solid Q3 2025 earnings report. I like the company's strong expected double-digit revenue growth for 2025 and 2026. I also like the net margin gains that were achieved in Q3. However, I still have concerns that the valuation is too high, which may cause the stock to underperform until this condition changes for the better. The technical outlook also looks bearish. As a result, I am going to lower my rating for the stock to a 'HOLD'.

data.title: DMB: Federally Tax-Exempt Income But Limited Appeal
data.description:
Overview

BNY Mellon Municipal Bond Infrastructure Fund (DMB) operates as a closed end fund that aims to provide attractive total returns by investing in a diverse range of municipal bonds that are federally tax-exempt. The fund invests in securities related to different sub-sectors of infrastructure across America. The fund essentially allows investors to be on the receiving end of these projects, while limiting their tax liability.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, DMB's share price has declined by about 1.03%. The share price was able to participate in the market rally over the last few months and mostly recovered from its large decline earlier in the year. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 3.36% over the same time frame. DMB now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 4.7% and issues payouts on a monthly basis. However, I worry about the sustainability of payouts going forward.

Due to the higher interest rate environment, DMB's performance and valuation have remained suppressed. Due to the exposure to the longer-dated maturities, the fund demonstrates a continued sensitivity to higher interest rates. So although there were some recent cuts, the federal funds rate still remains near the higher end of its ten-year range. So despite DMB trading at an attractive discount to NAV valuation, there are some clear weaknesses that limit the appeal right now. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that DMB implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the most recent fact sheet, DMB has total managed assets of $330.8M that is spread across a diverse range of municipal bonds. The fund has a listed expense ratio of 3.26%, but this is a bit misleading because it includes fees related to interest and other operating costs. When excluding these costs, the actual management fee sits at 0.65% of assets. As implied by the fund's name, DMB puts a specific emphasis on infrastructure related securities, which contribute to the rebuilding of America's roads, bridges, and common projects.

DMB invests in bonds that generate their yields through things like taxes or tolls. When assessing the fund's allocation by industry, we can see that DMB is most heavily allocated to Health Care securities at 24.63% of assets. This is followed by exposure to Transportation and Education securities, which account for 16.29% and 15.57% of assets, respectively. The combination of these top three industries make up over 56% of total assets, so investors should anticipate that these three industries will have the greatest impact on DMB's total return potential.

An important aspect of the fund's strategy is that DMB's portfolio is primarily allocated to long-dated bonds. As we can see below. 58.67% of its positions are allocated to securities with maturity dates that are 20+ years out. These longer maturity bonds are sensitive to interest rate changes, but they also ensure that the fund doesn't need to constantly rotate its positions around frequently. These longer dated maturity municipal bonds are typical with infrastructure related securities since they are typically longer-term projects.

Valuation

Since DMB is structured as a closed end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of its underlying assets. As a result, DMB now trades at a discount to NAV of 10.03%. For reference, DMB has traded at an average discount to NAV of 6.33% over the last five years. To better visualize the current discount to NAV in relation to DMB's historical range, I added the red line to the chart below. As we can see, DMB's current discount to NAV sits at one of the largest levels over the last decade, which can be an indication that it's a good time to accumulate.

However, I reviewed the latest semi-annual report to get some better clarity into the fund's internal performance. Net investment income amounted to $0.25 per share, while net realized losses landed at $1.13 per share. Assuming a similar performance over the remainder of the year, DMB is aligned to generate net investment income that is on par with its prior years. This can be an indication that the fund's strategy is efficient at generating income through different market environments. However, net investment income isn't always strong enough to support distributions on its own.

Therefore, DMB can sometimes be reliant on net realized gains to bridge the gap in earnings. Unfortunately, the fund has been unable to capture a meaningful level of net realized gains in recent years. DMB may be reliant on positive market momentum, which allows the fund to sell securities at a higher valuation than they were purchased for. This disappointing performance has ultimately stopped the fund from experiencing healthy NAV growth over time.

  • 2021: $14.41 per share
  • 2022: $13.86 per share
  • 2023: $11.70 per share
  • 2024: $12.06 per share
  • 2025: $12.11 per share
  • 2026 (semi-annual): $11.02 per share

Performance Risk

Investors need to remember that DMB prioritizes income generation, so investors should limit their expectations around the fund's ability to provide growth over time. To demonstrate this weakness, I pulled in a performance chart over the last ten years. As we can see below, DMB's share price has declined by 23.32% over the last decade. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return is offset to 0.79%. However, this disappointing performance may be attributed to the higher interest rate environment.

Additionally, the fund utilizes leverage in an effort to amplify its earnings when attractive opportunities arise. The use of leverage currently amounts to 35.52%, which is equivalent to nearly $43M. I consider this to be an aggressive use of leverage during this higher interest rate environment, and it can drastically increase the risk of the fund. Higher interest rates directly impact the operating spread between the cost of the fund's leverage and the yield collected from securities.

Additionally, DMB's structure means that the fund shares an inverse relationship with the federal funds rate. In short, when rates rise, the existing bond prices fall over time since newly issued bonds come out with higher yields. So despite the Fed initiating its second 25 basis point cut to interest rates, they still remain near the higher end of their ten-year range. As a result, we can see how DMB's share price has remained suppressed over recent years and struggles to regain enough momentum to get back to its pre-pandemic levels.

On a more positive note, DMB allocates the majority of its assets into positions with strong credit ratings. For reference, credit ratings above a BBB are classified as investment grade and have the lowest risk of being impacted by defaults. Less than 12% of the fund's assets are allocated to securities with below investment grade credit ratings. The breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 77.33%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 11.32%
  • Not Rated: 11.35%

Dividend

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.042 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 4.7%. If we take this monthly distribution and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of $0.504 per share. Referring back to the same semi-annual report, we can see that DMB has struggled to consistently generate earnings that exceed the distributions that are paid. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2021: ($0.01) per share
  • 2022: $0.09 per share
  • 2023: ($1.58) per share
  • 2024: $0.80 per share
  • 2025: $0.41 per share
  • 2026 (semi-annual): ($0.88) per share

As we can see, DMB has only generated earnings that exceed the payout in one of the last five years, excluding the most recent semi-annual period. Looking at the payout history below, the fund's payouts have started to decline over time. If performance doesn't improve over the next twelve months, I anticipate that another dividend cut may occur. For this reason, it becomes difficult to recommend a position in DMB, despite the potential tax benefits.

To clarify, DMB invests in municipal bonds that are exempt from federal income taxes. So the income received will not have any federal income taxes due. This increases the fund's flexibility to be used outside of traditional tax-advantaged accounts. While this may limit the tax consequences, investors should still be aware that distributions received may still suffer from some state taxes. So if you want to ensure that distributions have limited tax consequences, a position in DMB would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that DMB is only a hold for now. Despite the deep discount to NAV price range, I believe that the higher interest rate environment ultimately limits the appeal. As long as interest rates remain elevated, the fund's growth potential and earnings are limited. Additionally, the fund has displayed a lackluster ability at generating earnings that exceed the distributions being paid. As a result, DMB's NAV has declined over recent years. Despite the federally tax exempt nature of the distributions, I believe that the appeal is limited right now.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 11/22/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: Antero Resources: Acquisitions Increase Its Q4 2025 Production Expectations
data.description:
Antero Resources (AR) delivered roughly flat quarter-over-quarter production growth in Q3 2025 along with $70 million in free cash flow (before working capital changes). This was in-line with expectations.

Antero made several acquisitions that will boost its Q4 2025 production by 2% to 3%, and its Q4 2025 free cash flow should also be significantly higher (estimated at $245 million) due to improved natural gas prices.

With the current 2026 natural gas strip at over $4.15, Antero Resources has the potential to deliver further improvements in free cash flow next year, even with oil and C3+ NGL prices being relatively weak.

I am thus slightly increasing my estimated value for Antero to account for improved 2026 strip prices. I now estimate Antero's value at $37.50 to $38.50 per share in the long term (after 2026) with $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

Results For Q3 2025

Antero reported 3.429 Bcfe per day in Q3 2025 production, which was roughly flat compared to its Q2 2025 production. Antero's liquids production was approximately 206,000 barrels per day, which was a 3% quarter-over-quarter increase. This was driven by higher ethane production, though, while the more valuable liquids (oil and C3+ NGLs) saw some quarter-over-quarter production declines.

Antero reported $70 million in free cash flow before working capital changes in the quarter. This should be the low quarter of the year in terms of free cash flow, though, due to some seasonal weakness in natural gas prices.

Antero repurchased another 1.5 million shares in Q3 2025 for $51 million, bringing its year-to-date share repurchases up to 4.7 million shares for $163 million.

Acquisitions And Impact On Q4 2025 Results

Antero made three separate West Virginia acquisitions for a combined acquisition cost of approximately $260 million. These acquisitions give Antero another 75 to 100 MMCFE per day in net production as well as 10 net undeveloped locations.

Antero noted that the acquired assets are expected to generate over $50 million in 2026 free cash flow. The transactions closed near the end of Q3 2025, so it had minimal impact on Antero's Q3 production results but will fully impact its Q4 2025 production results.

As a result of the acquisitions, Antero now expects its full-year 2025 production to end up near the high end of its 3.4 to 3.45 Bcfe per day guidance. Without the acquisitions, Antero would likely be on track to end up near the midpoint of that guidance.

Antero also expects its Q4 2025 production to average between 3.5 to 3.525 Bcfe per day now. This would be a roughly 2% to 3% increase compared to its Q3 2025 production. Without the acquisitions, quarter-over-quarter production growth would be relatively flat.

Potential Q4 2025 Outlook

I've modeled out Antero's potential Q4 2025 results below, based on the current strip of approximately $3.80 NYMEX natural gas.

Antero may be able to generate $1.31 billion in revenues after hedges for Q4 2025.

Type Barrels/Mcf $ Per Barrel/Mcf $ Million
Natural Gas 208,932,000 $3.95

$825
Ethane 7,820,000 $11.50 $90
C3+ NGLs 10,580,000 $34.00 $360
Oil 644,000 $46.00 $30
Hedge Value -$6
Distributions To Martica -$20
Antero Midstream Dividends $31
Total $1,310

Antero may thus generate around $245 million in Q4 2025 free cash flow, helped by realized natural gas prices that may be improved by approximately $0.80 per Mcf compared to Q3 2025. This would be its second-best quarter of 2025 in terms of free cash flow

Expenses $ Million
Cash Production and Marketing Expense $820
Cash G&A $40
Cash Interest $5
Capital Expenditures $200
Total Expenditures $1,065

Antero has also added a spot rig in Q4 2025 to spud a dry gas pad. This additional activity is covered within its existing 2025 budget.

Antero hasn't drilled a dry gas Marcellus pad in over a decade, but it is using this as a proof of concept for potentially supplying natural gas to power data centers or to supply other power generation projects. Antero noted that it has over 1,000 dry gas locations if market conditions warrant development.

Notes on Valuation

Antero's Q3 2025 results were solid and in-line with expectations, while its Q4 2025 free cash flow should be noticeably improved due to higher natural gas prices.

The natural gas strip for 2026 is currently relatively strong (at $4.15 to $4.20), which should lead to further improvements in Antero's free cash flow next year. Liquids prices are relatively weak, but natural gas prices are more important, and natural gas should account for around 65% of Antero's revenues next year.

I have thus increased my estimate of Antero's value by $0.50 per share to reflect the improved projections around its 2026 free cash flow.

I now estimate Antero's value at $37.50 to $38.50 per share in a long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas scenario.

Conclusion

Antero Resources has continued to perform decently, with production meeting expectations in Q3 2025. It made some acquisitions that will add around 2% to 3% to its production starting in Q4 2025, but otherwise its production expectations are unchanged.

Antero's free cash flow (before working capital changes) should ramp back up after dropping to $70 million in Q3 2025. While oil and C3+ NGLs prices are weaker now, the outlook for natural gas prices looks positive. Natural gas strip prices are currently around $3.80 in Q4 2025 and over $4.15 for 2026.

I have slightly increased my estimate of Antero's value to $37.50 to $38.50 per share in a long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas scenario. This reflects an improvement to its outlook for free cash flow in 2026.

data.title: FIGB: Attractive Take On The Aggregate Bond Index
data.description:
Thesis

This year has been very interesting so far, with very few asset classes outperforming. While some investors might see the lofty figures associated with the S&P 500 for the year, it is not all milk and honey when it comes to equities in 2025:

While the S&P 500 has done well, the equally weighted Invesco S&P 500 ETF (RSP) is up only 3.9%, while the small-cap fund iShares Russell 2000 (IWM) is up only 3.6%. On a risk-adjusted basis, these figures are quite poor.

On the other hand, investment-grade bonds have done very well this year, with the iShares Core U.S. Aggregate Bond ETF (AGG) up over 6.8% this year on a total return basis.

In today's article we are going to discuss an interesting exchange-traded fund from Fidelity that represents an active take on AGG. We shall outline the composition for the Fidelity Investment Grade Bond ETF (FIGB), its structure and performance, and highlight why we find the fund to be an attractive choice in today's environment.

What does FIGB do?

Let us start by looking at the fund's own definitions:

Benchmarked against the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, the fund seeks to deliver competitive risk-adjusted performance that is commensurate with investor expectations of a core bond fund.

FIGB is an active take on AGG, although a low-touch one. Its current portfolio closely shadows what the aggregate index does:

Please note the fund provides a column for its portfolio weight to each asset segment, and a relative weight, which represents the difference versus the index. The fund is currently overweight U.S. Treasuries, ABS bonds, and cash, and underweight MBS bonds. As an active ETF, the name will choose to adjust each large segment as per its own views.

The ratings matrix provides the same information in terms of basis to the index:

Please note FIGB having a larger BBB allocation when compared to the overall index. The fund will keep adjusting its holdings as per the portfolio managers' views in order to outperform AGG and take advantage of specific market positioning.

Some general fund analytics are as follows:

  • AUM: $0.28 bil
  • Expense ratio: 0.36%
  • Annualized volatility (Seeking Alpha): 5.7%
  • SEC yield: 4.2%
  • Duration: 6.1 years

Performance

Let us start by looking at the AGG performance throughout time:

  • 2018: 0.1%
  • 2019: 8.4%
  • 2020: 7.4%
  • 2021: -1.7%
  • 2022: -13.0%
  • 2023: 5.6%
  • 2024: 1.3%
  • 2025: 6.8%

As we can see from the above table, AGG does best in a decreasing rate environment, with robust figures posted in 2019, 2020, and so far in 2025. As a fixed income instrument with a duration component, the fund posts negative figures when rates rise.

Now let us see FIGB's total return versus AGG since its inception:

FIGB is represented by the teal line and has displayed a slightly better overall performance, although the basis is fairly slim.

When compared to a cohort of peers, FIGB does well but is not the outlier:

The PIMCO Active Bond ETF (BOND) is the outperformer here, with the rest of the names very close to each other. Please note we are using a 1-year time frame for this comparison because not all funds were in the market for a longer time-frame.

Interest rate outlook

As we saw from the above analysis, FIGB is set to do well when interest rates decrease. We have been told numerous times by the office of the president that interest rates are set to move lower, and the market is pricing that as such:

The current SOFR forward curve is showing us the floating rate bottoming close to 3% in very early 2027, with a steep move lower in 2026. Market participants are therefore currently pricing a 100 bps cut in fed funds in 2026.

While FIGB has an intermediate duration, the belly of the curve is fairly well correlated with the front end, so we should see a 50 bps move lower there as well. That should translate into a 3% gain for FIGB given its duration profile. So as per the current pricing and portfolio yield, we should expect another approximate +7% gain for the ETF in 2026, coming from duration and portfolio yield.

While the long end of the curve is very stubbornly high, we are of the opinion that even surprisingly sticky inflation will not derail interest rate cuts, especially given the presidential mandate to appoint a new Fed chair in May 2026.

Active versus passive take

In years when rates move around, active takes on a portfolio are better than passive indexing. Why? Because volatility always brings overbought/oversold conditions for assets, an active manager can take advantage of clear market signals with respect to a balanced portfolio.

Active management does not mean that an exhaustive bottoms up analysis needs to be performed, but simply understanding when market participants are too exuberant about rate cuts for a certain Fed meeting or, conversely, when they are too pessimistic about inflationary pressures. Gentle adjustments in the portfolio segmentation and duration can bring alpha for an active fund.

Conclusion

FIGB is an active ETF that aims to shadow the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. The name has provided alpha against the index, with a slightly larger total return over time. FIGB has an intermediate duration of 6.1 years, and is set to gain further as rates move lower. We like FIGB in the context of today's macro, and we would rather buy an active fund versus a passive one here.

data.title: Earnings Euphoria Vs. Market Reality: Nvidia's Reversal, Fed Uncertainty, And The Volatility Trade
data.description:
By Ryan J. Puplava, CMT, CTS, CES

Nvidia’s Earnings Blowout and Reversal

Nvidia’s (NVDA) earnings on Wednesday evening were one of the most anticipated corporate events of the month. Sentiment around the AI trade had weakened heading into the release, and the broader semiconductor complex had been under heavy pressure for several sessions.

When Nvidia surpassed expectations and issued strong fourth quarter guidance, the initial reaction on Thursday morning was overwhelmingly positive. The stock climbed nearly five percent at the open, and the PHLX Semiconductor Index surged close to three percent. The broader market rallied sharply as well, with all eleven S&P 500 sectors trading higher for a period and the major indices briefly reclaiming their 50-day moving averages.

Yet the tide reversed quickly. After hitting a peak just before 11:00 Eastern Time, stocks began to roll over. What started as normal profit taking in the mega-cap space broadened into selling across nearly every major sector.

By the close, Nvidia had declined more than three percent and semiconductors had fallen almost five percent, flipping what had been a strong rebound into one of the sharpest intraday reversals of the quarter.

Other chipmakers such as Micron (MU) and AMD (AMD) also saw steep losses. The full reversal underscored how fragile sentiment had become around AI leadership. Despite stellar fundamentals, investors remain concerned about the durability of data center spending, elevated valuations, and the concentration of market returns within a narrow segment of mega-cap technology.

The reversal also reflected a broader issue in the week’s trading. From Monday through Thursday, every attempt to regain upside momentum was met with selling pressure. Movements in the VIX above 22 suggested heightened uncertainty, and heavyweight sectors like information technology repeatedly failed to hold early gains.

Nvidia became the clearest expression of this dynamic. Market participants embraced the strength of its results but were unwilling to extend risk exposure after a multi-month run in AI oriented names.

Investment Implications: A strong earnings backdrop is not sufficient to drive performance when sentiment is fragile and valuations are extended. Investors may benefit from more balanced exposure between cyclical sectors, defensive areas, and select technology names rather than relying on concentrated AI leadership. The volatility in semiconductors highlights the value of incremental position sizing and disciplined risk management.

Rate Cut Expectations and Cloudy Data

Federal Reserve communication and mixed economic releases were the second major driver of market volatility this week. Rate cut expectations for the December meeting swung dramatically, starting near 45 percent on Monday, rising modestly on Tuesday, dropping sharply on Wednesday, and then jumping back toward 70 percent on Friday.

Wednesday’s sharp decline in cut expectations was triggered by two unexpected events. The Bureau of Labor Statistics announced a delay in releasing the October Employment Situation Report and confirmed that the September JOLTS report would not be published.

Labor market data has been the most important factor shaping the Fed’s recent dovish tilt, so withholding key indicators introduced uncertainty. The market interpreted the lack of data as a complication for policymakers who prefer a cautious approach as they evaluate the risk of cutting too early. The CME FedWatch Tool reflected this shift, with expectations for a December cut falling to roughly 32 percent by Wednesday afternoon.

The minutes from the October FOMC meeting reinforced the sense that the committee was divided. Some participants favored a cut for risk management reasons, while others felt the policy rate should remain unchanged through the end of the year. Nearly all emphasized the need to proceed carefully as employment softness emerges and inflation progress continues unevenly.

By Friday, however, sentiment swung back in the opposite direction. New York Fed President John Williams said he sees room for further adjustment in the near term, suggesting a willingness to bring policy closer to neutral.

These comments sharply boosted cut expectations, lifting the probability of a December reduction to nearly 75 percent at the highs before moderating slightly late in the session. Boston Fed President Collins also noted that mildly restrictive policy remains appropriate, which helped maintain the market’s dovish interpretation even though her comments were less forceful.

Economic releases throughout the week painted a complicated picture. Initial jobless claims were low (232k). The Empire State Manufacturing Index surprised to the upside, while factory orders posted a solid rebound with new orders showing back-to-back increases in July and August.

Construction spending improved modestly and the housing market showed some stabilization as lower mortgage rates aided October sales. The mixed nature of the data reinforced the idea that the economy is slowing but not collapsing, which sets the stage for meaningful debate inside the Fed heading into December.

Investment Implications: The sharp swings in rate expectations increase the value of diversification across interest-sensitive sectors. Financials, homebuilders, rate exposed tech, utilities, and consumer discretionary stocks may all react differently to shifting Fed probabilities. Allocations should consider the potential for continued volatility in fixed income and equity markets as the December meeting approaches.

Market Leadership, Breadth, and Rotation

The third key theme was the persistent instability in market leadership. Each day saw wide divergences among sectors. Early in the week, defensive groups such as utilities and health care attracted inflows as semiconductors and mega cap technology sold off.

Communication services repeatedly bucked weakness in other tech segments as Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) extended its leadership. Energy saw modest strength midweek as crude oil prices rebounded. On Tuesday, health care benefited from strong earnings and dividend announcements from Medtronic (MDT) and Merck (MRK), while consumer discretionary lagged sharply following Home Depot’s (HD) guidance cut.

By Thursday, most sectors succumbed to broad selling despite strong early gains. Only consumer staples held a positive finish. Friday shifted the tone again as rate cut expectations surged and the market embraced a risk-on posture. Small caps outperformed, materials and consumer discretionary posted gains above two percent mid-day, and homebuilders rallied more than five percent mid-day.

Breadth was inconsistent throughout the week, with frequent reversals that reflected uncertain positioning. Even on days when indices were mixed, the underlying rotation was often pronounced. This created challenging conditions for trend-following investors, while stock pickers benefited from relative strength in more stable, value-oriented areas.

Investment Implications: Persistent sector rotation favors diversified portfolios with exposure to both cyclical and defensive segments. Investors should consider rebalancing as correlations shift and as mega-cap concentration risk remains elevated.

Chart of the Week

The volatility this week means it’s time to look at some breadth/sentiment indicators to see how far investors have swung bearish. Usually, extremes can help pinpoint potential reversal opportunities for volatility and opportunities for bulls to step in.

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) has been called the “beta on beta” play and often turns before other risk assets. It’s a good idea to keep a good eye on it. The long-term trend from 2022 to 2025 has broken, with its first level of support tested this week.

The next level lower would be near the 200-day moving average and former area of congestion around $60k. Additionally, a rally back up to the 50-day moving average and a reversal there could spell more structural signs of a long-term top.

Source: StockCharts, Ryan Puplava, CMT CTS CES

The percentage of stocks above the 50-day moving average within the S&P 500 has been in decline since the summer. We aren’t quite in oversold territory yet, and the trend shows no change at the moment.

Source: StockCharts, Ryan Puplava, CMT CTS CES

The Volatility Index uses call and put options, out-of-money options and SPX options to figure out how much implied volatility investors think is being priced in the market. I divide the levels of the VIX Index basically into Defcon levels (1-4), with 1 being the worst and something we experienced back during Covid and again last April.

We basically hit Defcon 3, which is a moderate level and one that has seen bulls step in during minor corrections. If investors get more nervous, a breach above 30 on the VIX Index could happen, but I don’t think the catalysts I’ve read in the news justify that kind of risk-off thinking.

Source: StockCharts, Ryan Puplava, CMT CTS CES

Conclusion

The week illustrated how sensitive the market has become to both earnings expectations and shifting monetary policy assumptions. Nvidia delivered outstanding results but could not sustain gains because sentiment toward AI leadership remains fragile.

At the same time, a lack of clarity around labor market data and mixed Fed communication drove large swings in rate expectations. Sector rotation and inconsistent breadth added to the volatility.

As the market approaches the December FOMC meeting and moves deeper into year-end, a balanced approach that emphasizes diversification, risk discipline, and selective exposure appears prudent.

Original Post

data.title: Farmers & Merchants Bancorp: Record Earnings Meets A Historically Cheap Valuation
data.description:
Farmers & Merchants Bancorp (OTCQX:FMCB) has remained a fairly quiet performer in recent months, delivering a roughly flat return since my last update in the summer. While that is consistent with the returns of the wider small bank space, this Lodi, California-based player has been treading water for a few years now – a frustrating performance given the extent of its earnings and balance sheet growth in that time.

At the business level, there remains very little to complain about. Although economic uncertainty persists, Farmers served up a record set of earnings in the first nine months of 2025, while strong credit quality, healthy pre-provision earnings, and abundant capital will stand it in good stead if the economy does deteriorate.

Farmers offers investors a compelling mix of quality and value. It wouldn't be hyperbolic to describe the bank as one of the best in America, and yet, its shares continue to hit new lows relative to book value and earnings. The kicker is that these shares trade on the pink sheets, so they're only suitable for patient retail investors happy to deal in small sizes. If that applies to readers, then these shares could appeal. I remain bullish.

Posting Record Profits

Farmers has continued to move in the right direction since my last piece, with the bank posting earnings per share of $33.92 in the third quarter, up 13% year-on-year and 3% quarter-on-quarter. This performance takes year-to-date EPS to $99.67, up 11% year-on-year and a new nine-month record for the bank.

Farmers generates around 90% of its revenue from net interest income ('NII'), so trends here are critical to its performance. NII clocked in at $55.4 million last quarter, up 6.5% year-on-year and 3% quarter-on-quarter. This was driven by a higher net interest margin - up 9 bps year-on-year and the same quarter-on-quarter to 4.16% - as well as balance sheet growth, with interest-earning assets clocking in at $5.3 billion, up 4% year-on-year and flat quarter-on-quarter.

Because there aren't really any incremental expenses associated with NIM-fueled growth, it tends to drive a lot of operating leverage. Said in plainer English, it often produces even faster earnings growth. This was on display last quarter, with Farmers' pre-provision income rising over 9% year-on-year, around 260 bps ahead of NII growth.

Now, interest rates were relatively static over the first three quarters of the year. The Fed did cut once in that time, but it was close to the end of last quarter. Since then, it has delivered one 25 bps cut in Q4, with another one looking possible in the December meeting.

For Farmers, there are various puts and takes to consider with this. On the liability side, it already enjoys rock-bottom funding costs. Not only is Farmers virtually 100% deposit funded, but its deposits were paying an annualized rate of just 1.22% last quarter. Related to that, around 32% of its deposits are in accounts that pay no interest at all.

Since its funding costs are already so low, it can be difficult to extract more from this when interest rates are falling. Consistent with this, Farmer's average cost of deposits is relatively unchanged since Q1 2024, despite the clear decline in short-term interest rates.

On the asset side, Farmers' lending does skew nearly 60% to fixed-rate loans. Its variable-rate loans also have floors that will limit the downside from lower rates. In addition, management moved around 40% of the bank's cash into investment securities last quarter, reducing its sensitivity to rate changes.

Weighing all of that up, Farmers' latest NII sensitivity model had it positioned fairly neutral, as shown above. Accordingly, I would be looking for the bank's to NIM to hold up relatively well, leaving NII growth a function of volume growth.

A Good Pick For Economic Uncertainty

As a group, smaller banks have underperformed the S&P 500 by around ten points in the past couple of months. I believe credit concerns are partly to blame, with a number of regional banks reporting losses linked to fraud. The market may be nervous that the credit cycle is finally beginning to turn against the industry.

With this in mind, Farmers represents a solid pick. First and foremost, its loan book skews almost 30% towards agricultural lending. The key point I would make about this is that credit quality in this part of the loan book won't really follow the typical economic/business cycle. Indeed, none of Farmers' agricultural loans were past due at the end of last quarter, while just 0.03% of its total loans were delinquent.

If credit losses do rise from here, well, Farmers has a very profitable underlying business from which it can absorb higher provisions. Indeed, it has already generated $98.7 million in pre-provision income through the first three quarters of the year, equal to circa 2.7% of its loan book. Plus, Farmers has already set aside the equivalent of 2% of its loans in its loss reserve.

Given the above lines of defense, I find it very unlikely that Farmers' capital would come under threat, even in a severe recession. However, should it come to it, Farmers has an extremely strong balance sheet, with the bank sitting on a 14.28% CET1 ratio at the end of Q3. This is up 40 bps since my last piece, leaving Farmers with a $325 million cushion over its regulatory minimum.

The Valuation Slips Even Further

While things continue to look fine at the business level, Farmers' valuation has continued to slip. Its stock has basically gone nowhere since my last update - trading for $1,025 as I type - while it tangible book value has risen 5% to $877.13. As a result, Farmers' P/TBV ratio has slipped to just 1.17x.

In the introduction, I said that this trend has been ongoing for a few years. Farmers' share price has remained relatively flat, yet its earnings and tangible book value have been growing nicely.

This has resulted in significant valuation compression. As you can see, not only are the stock's P/TBV and P/E ratios at steep discounts to their 10-year averages, but they are actually at 10-year lows.

Frankly, these multiples are very cheap. Farmers could offer investors a 13% annualized return by simply spending all of its net income on dividends. The only real explanation I have for this is that its shares trade over-the-counter, where volume is thin and price discovery can be a long process. While that has led to a frustrating period for shareholders, it has also opened up a great long-term buying opportunity. Since there is nothing wrong with its underlying business, I am leaving my Buy rating for Farmers & Merchants Bancorp in place.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: MTUM: $20B Momentum ETF Not The Best Choice In Its Category
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The iShares MSCI USA Momentum Factor ETF (MTUM) may have attracted nearly $20B in assets under management, but my review suggests it's not the best large-cap momentum ETF on the market right now. In the article below, I will compare its performance and fundamentals with the Invesco S&P 500 Momentum ETF (SPMO) and three newcomers to the category, and use them to explain why I wouldn't put MTUM at the top of your "buy" list anytime soon. I hope you enjoy the read.

iShares MSCI USA Momentum Factor ETF Overview

MTUM Strategy Discussion

Since December 1, 2020, MTUM has tracked the MSCI USA Momentum SR Variant Index after previously tracking the MSCI USA Momentum Index. Often, a change in Index renders past performance less relevant, but in this case, the main change revolved around the timing of the rebalancing process. For full details, please feel free to read this news release that states how both Indexes "will generally invest in the same securities."

According to the methodology document, the Index "is designed to represent the performance of a strategy that seeks higher exposure to a momentum factor. MSCI calculates momentum for all constituents in the MSCI USA Index based on their six- and twelve-month price returns (excluding the most recent month), and then calculates risk-adjusted momentum by dividing those values by the weekly standard deviation of returns over the last three years. The six- and twelve-month risk-adjusted momentum scores are combined at equal weight, and the Index selects the top 125 for inclusion in the Index.

The Index weights each security by the product of its momentum score and its market capitalization. In addition, extra constraints are applied, such as:

  • Single security weights are capped at 5%.
  • The sum of securities with weights above 4.5% is capped at 22.5%.
  • Turnover is capped at 30% at each quarterly review.

The turnover limit is an interesting constraint, and in fast-changing markets, it's possible MTUM could get caught holding many stocks whose momentum has either stalled or is trending downward. At 30%, it could take a couple of quarters to correct, though ideally, considering short-term momentum (six months) alongside long-term momentum (twelve months) should help.

MTUM Fund Facts and Characteristics

The following is a list of fund facts and characteristics for MTUM, pulled from its website, fact sheet, latest annual financial statements, and quote page on Seeking Alpha.

  • Ticker: MTUM
  • Price: $238.02
  • Exchange: Cboe BZX
  • Benchmark Index: MSCI USA Momentum SR Variant Index
  • Inception Date: April 16, 2013
  • Net Assets: $19.6B
  • Expense Ratio: 0.15%
  • 30 Day Median Bid/Ask Spread: 0.02%
  • Premium / Discount: -0.01%
  • Dividend Yield: 0.96%
  • Dividend Frequency: Quarterly
  • Portfolio Turnover Rate: 116% (95-125% from 2021-2024)

A Quick Correction: MTUM Does Not Use Representative Sampling

Briefly, I've read several other Seeking Alpha articles claiming that MTUM uses a "representative sampling" strategy. This statement is incorrect and appears to stem from a quick reading of the clause below from MTUM's prospectus.

BFA uses a representative sampling indexing strategy to manage the Fund. "Representative sampling" is an indexing strategy that involves investing in a representative sample of securities that collectively has an investment profile similar to that of an applicable underlying index. The securities selected are expected to have, in the aggregate, investment characteristics (based on factors such as market capitalization and industry weightings), fundamental characteristics (such as return variability and yield) and liquidity measures similar to those of an applicable underlying index. The Fund may or may not hold all of the securities in the Underlying Index.

Readers should know that this clause is boilerplate for nearly all BlackRock U.S. Equity ETFs, including the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), which is certainly fully replicated. Furthermore, MTUM currently holds 125 companies, which is the same number as the Index, so while the statement gives the Fund the right to sample, perhaps for liquidity or cash flow management purposes, in practice, it does not. That's why the difference between MTUM's annual returns and that of the Index is consistently low and nearly fully explained by the fund's 0.15% expense ratio (14.93% vs. 15.15% since its inception as of October 31, 2025).

Sector Allocations and Top Ten Holdings

As of November 19, 2025, MTUM allocates 32.83% of its assets to stocks in the Technology sector, followed by Financials, Industrials, and Communication Services at 17.63%, 12.33%, and 11.47%, respectively. The ETF has minimal exposure to Energy stocks, but these allocations are subject to change. For example, archived versions of MTUM's website showed its Energy allocation was 17% midway through 2022, while Health Care and Energy combined for nearly 60% in early 2023.

MTUM's top ten holdings are listed next, which comprise 42% of the portfolio. Notice how Broadcom (AVGO) and JPMorgan Chase (JPM) have exceeded the 5% single security cap due to their strong returns since the last quarterly rebalance. Nvidia (NVDA), Palantir Technologies (PLTR), and Microsoft (MSFT) round out MTUM's top five.

iShares MSCI USA Momentum Factor ETF Performance Analysis

The table below highlights the periodic returns for 21 large-cap momentum ETFs, five of which (including MTUM) have a ten-year track record. As shown, MTUM's 291.74% ten-year total return as of October 31, 2025, ranks #2/5 but is well behind the Invesco S&P 500 Momentum ETF (SPMO). These two ETFs combine for $31.5B in assets under management, roughly 82% of the total, so needless to say, SPMO is MTUM's primary competitor.

However, MTUM's relative ranking has dropped as new ETFs have entered the market. For example, its 78.41% three-year total ranks #8/19, while its 90.31% five-year gain ranks #12/17. Here are the three that caught my eye, which I'll add to the fundamental analysis alongside MTUM and SPMO.

  • Fidelity Momentum Factor ETF (FDMO)
  • JPMorgan U.S. Momentum Factor ETF (JMOM)
  • SEI Enhanced U.S. Large Cap Momentum Factor ETF (SEIM)

iShares MSCI USA Momentum Factor ETF Fundamental Analysis

The following table highlights selected fundamental statistics for MTUM's top 15 holdings, which comprise 53.21% of the fund on a cash-adjusted basis. Please have a scan of the holdings and summary metrics for each ETF at the bottom, and I'll offer my takeaways afterward.

MTUM: Short-Term Price Momentum Is Relatively Weak

MTUM's Index considers both short-term (six months) and long-term (twelve months) price momentum. Therefore, I'm surprised to see its constituents are up only 16.05% over the last six months, the lowest in this sample. When taken alongside these same constituents' 40.83% one-year price gain, it's clear the selections are driven by price action that occurred 12-6 months ago rather than recent price changes. This could be due to the turnover constraint, which might limit the Index's ability to delete stocks like Meta Platforms (META) and Visa (V). We'll have to see next quarter.

On the other hand, FDMO and SEIM offer better short-term price momentum without compromising long-term momentum, whereas SPMO's Index only considers risk-adjusted twelve-month price returns, so it's no surprise to see it also lag behind over the short term.

MTUM: Competitive Growth and Value Mix

All five ETFs feature similar combinations of growth and value, with JMOM as a small outlier due to its lower growth profile. To illustrate, consider these modified PEG ratios by dividing each ETF's one-year estimated earnings growth rates into their forward P/E ratios:

  • MTUM: 25.51x / 23.32% = 1.09x
  • SPMO: 25.99x / 24.35% = 1.07x
  • FDMO: 26.06x / 22.35% = 1.17x
  • JMOM: 23.90x / 19.92% = 1.20x
  • SEIM: 25.48x / 24.49% = 1.04x

These ETFs might be considered expensive, but when you factor in growth, they're actually pretty good deals, with SEIM technically offering the best combination. Still, neither fund should be considered a "one-trick pony" or a single-factor fund, so I don't think shareholders need to panic even when the momentum factor falls out of favor.

MTUM: Great Profit Margins But With High Leverage

MTUM's EBIT margins are excellent at 29.80%, though its 16.60% return on total capital is on the low end, and its 1.26x weighted average debt/equity ratio is quite high relative to its peers. This is where I think SPMO stands out -its margins are higher across the board (gross, EBITDA, EBIT, net, and free cash flow), and its 17.68% ROTC, which explicitly ignores leverage, is second only to SEIM.

Investment Recommendation

MTUM is a well-established large-cap momentum ETF with $19.6B in assets under management and a low 0.15% expense ratio. Its Index considers both short- and long-term price momentum on a risk-adjusted basis, but applies some constraints to improve diversification, and limits turnover to just 30% each quarter, meaning it's possible MTUM will be slow to react in fast-changing markets. While many momentum strategies are vulnerable in those situations, I prefer SPMO, since the numbers indicate its balance sheet is stronger, its margins are higher, and its growth, value, and quality mix is just as good, if not better. I also assign some weight to SPMO's historical returns, where it has the edge over the last three, five, and ten years.

I also think some of the newer funds are interesting. In particular, FDMO and SEIM look good because they currently provide better short- and long-term price momentum exposure than MTUM and SPMO while offering good growth-at-a-reasonable-price. As a result, I think MTUM's share of the large-cap momentum ETF category will soon decline, and that the most appropriate rating today is a simple "hold." Thank you for reading, and I look forward to your comments below.

data.title: How Banks And Private Markets Are Redefining Credit
data.description:
By Louay Mikdashi & Jonathan Shahrabani

The U.S. banking industry is entering a new era in which regulated banks and private lenders are increasingly intertwined. We believe this trend will continue to open attractive opportunities for dynamic allocation across the credit spectrum.

In this article, we explore various aspects of the broader strategic realignment unfolding across the U.S. banking sector. Specifically, we address structural shifts in how credit is created (led by the increase in lending to non-depository financial institutions) and how risk is assumed (including further adoption of Significant Risk Transfer transactions). We also discuss the banking sector’s ongoing pursuit of stability, scale and durable earnings growth as private lenders assume a more active role in long-term financing.

This evolution is not zero-sum for investors, in our view. Rather, we believe it is widening the pipeline of opportunities across public and private credit markets.

Redefining Credit: Banks and NDFIs Come Together

The U.S. banking industry is entering a new era shaped by evolving regulation and shifting competitive dynamics. In the wake of the 2024 U.S. presidential election, policymakers have revisited several key elements of the post-crisis framework, including Basel 3 Endgame, the Supplementary Leverage Ratio and the Federal Reserve’s stress-testing process. While implementation timelines remain fluid, we believe the direction of reform is increasingly clear and headed toward a risk system that balances prudent oversight with greater operational flexibility and a more sensitive approach to capital.

These developments are unfolding alongside the continued rise of private markets. Rather than competing head-to-head, banks and private lenders are becoming increasingly connected through financing relationships, distribution arrangements and in some cases overlapping exposures. The result is a more integrated credit ecosystem in which both sides play complementary roles in capital formation.

Lending to non-depository financial institutions (NDFIs) remains the fastest-growing category on regulated bank balance sheets: Excluding this segment, overall loan growth would be flat for the industry (see figure 1). We believe this shift highlights how banks are pursuing areas of structural growth while developing a more symbiotic relationship with private lenders.

Figure 1: Loans to NDFIs have Driven Overall Growth in Bank Lending

Market behavior around the Tricolor, First Brands and Cantor losses in September and October underscored these linkages. Bank, BDC and alternative asset manager stocks moved in close correlation (see figure 2), reflecting perceived shared exposures and a broader sensitivity to credit conditions, even where exposure differed.

Figure 2: Bank, BDC and Alternative Asset Manager Stocks have been Moving Together

Despite the headlines and ensuing market volatility, our analysis suggests that the risk of further credit losses remains manageable. When we stress-tested NDFI loans across major U.S. banks at a 1% loss rate (versus most banks’ historical track record of near zero losses over the years for this segment of their lending book ), we found that potential losses would remain well within banks’ earnings capacity to absorb them (see figure 3).

Figure 3: Loans Across Major U.S. Banks Appear Manageable Assuming a 1% Loss Rate on NDFI Portfolios

For perspective, since 1985 the long-term charge-off rate on C&I loans has been 71 bps, and the highest quarterly charge-off ever recorded—in 3Q 2009, during the Global Financial Crisis—was 2.66% (see figure 4). In our view, conservative underwriting, meaningful collateral coverage and short-duration profiles continue to anchor the strength of these NDFI portfolios, and that recent concerns of broader systemic cracking are likely overstated.

Figure 4: Commercial and Industrial Loan Charge-offs have Averaged 71 bps Since 1985

Furthermore, the regulatory treatment for NDFI lending remains favorable. Under the Simplified Supervisory Formula Approach (SSFA), risk weights are determined based on the credit enhancement and subordination characteristics of a given exposure rather than by applying a uniform 100% treatment. As a result, loans to NDFIs often prove more capital efficient—with risk-weighted-asset values well below 100%—compared to other kinds of loans (see figure 5).

Figure 5: Under Current Regulation, NDFI Loans are Comparatively Capital Efficient

In light of modest historical losses and favorable regulatory treatment, we expect NDFI lending to remain a key source of incremental growth for the banking industry.

Expanding Cooperation: The Rise of Significant Risk Transfers

Alongside the growth in NDFI activity, banks and private lenders are cooperating on other fronts. One prominent example: Significant Risk Transfer (SRT) transactions, in which banks share portfolio exposure with external investors such as private funds and insurance vehicles.

SRT structures allow banks to manage regulatory capital efficiently while providing private-market participants with diversified access to institutional credit pools in a subordinated position. Growth in SRT activity (see figure 6) underscores how balance-sheet lending and alternative capital have become increasingly interdependent, linking regulatory capital management and private demand on a single continuum.

For investors, rising SRT supply has met significant demand, compressing yield spreads. While we believe traditional SRTs, which reference corporate loans, may still offer some relative value, those that offer exposure to differentiated credit pools—including residential and commercial mortgages, as well as project finance—may offer more attractive risk-adjusted returns, in our view.

Figure 6: Demand for Significant Risk Transfer (SRT) Transactions is on the Rise

SRT Volume

In parallel, several global banks are developing or expanding private market capabilities of their own, often through partnerships with established private market players. In our view, increased competition in some sectors for credit—and within corporate direct lending in particular—reinforces investors’ need to build a differentiated origination strategy to achieve a sustainable edge going forward.

The Quest for Durability: Banks Continue to Seek Scale and Stability

We believe deeper engagement with private markets is only one aspect of a broader strategic realignment across the banking sector. The largest institutions continue to allocate significant portions of excess capital to share repurchases and higher dividends—exactly what equity investors have rewarded, with the shares of the largest U.S. banks trading near all-time highs on valuation (see figure 7).

Figure 7: Shareholders have Rewarded Banks for Boosting Buybacks and Dividends

Source: FactSet and VisibleAlpha as of November 13, 2025 for U.S. GSIBs.

Among regional banks, consolidation remains a defining theme (see figure 8). Large regionals are acquiring smaller peers to increase scale, improve efficiency and strengthen their deposit franchises. We believe these actions aim to improve long-term competitiveness and cost discipline rather than to reclaim market share from private lenders. Across the industry, the focus remains on stability, scalability and durable earnings growth, in our view.

Figure 8: Consolidation is Picking Up Across the U.S. Banking Industry

Consolidation may have knock-on benefits for private markets, too. For example, private credit managers may be able to borrow at more attractive rates, fueling even greater NDFI activity. Additional consolidation could also trigger the disposal of non-core assets, offering potentially compelling investments for well-positioned managers.

Conclusion: How to Capitalize on Credit Market Convergence

The post-2024 landscape is reshaping how credit is created, transferred and held. Banks are recalibrating balance sheets for efficiency while private lenders are assuming a more active role in long-term financing. We believe the result is not a zero-sum shift but an expanding opportunity set across the credit spectrum.

For investors, this evolution is tangible. SRT transactions and related bank risk-transfer structures provide direct, institutional access to high-quality, granular exposures once confined to bank portfolios. At the same time, broader cooperation in origination, syndication and distribution is widening the pipeline of investable credit assets.

Within this environment, we believe multi-credit platforms could be attractively positioned, allowing investors to allocate dynamically across public and private channels; participate in both bank-linked and direct-lending activity; and capture yield premia created by regulatory and structural dislocations.

At Neuberger, we are seeing this convergence in real time, and that the integration of regulated and private capital is not merely cyclical, but rather represents a durable, structural evolution in global markets. For diversified private credit investors, we believe the next phase of growth will be defined not by competition with banks, but by cooperation that opens greater opportunity.
